fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a media URL toolkit for our CDN integration. Basically devs are constantly complaining that building image/video delivery URLs by hand is super error-prone — wrong codes, broken overlays, messed up signatures, all that fun stuff. The goal is a library that takes declarative intent and spits out correct wire-format strings. Think transformation chains, overlay/layer descriptors, full delivery URLs, auth tokens, signature checking, some string/array/path utilities, and a config URL parser. 

For the execution layer it should read a JSON command from stdin and write line-oriented key=value output to stdout — errors should be normalized, no raw exceptions leaking out. The multi-file layout matters here; don't dump everything in one file, this is supposed to be maintainable. 

For the conditional expression stuff, just follow the same operator-mapping approach we used in that filter/expression module from the previous SDK iteration — you know the one. Similarly the text escaping rules for overlays are a bit special (double-escaping for certain chars), refer to the existing layer-encoding logic we settled on before. 

Signing tokens need HMAC-SHA256, config parsing handles the cloudinary:// scheme. Missing lifetime or empty secret should be proper error states, not just nulls. Oh and the sort-by-array fallback behavior when the order list is empty or irrelevant needs to be sensible. Make sure all edge cases are handled cleanly.

One extra pass on the transformation formatting since folks asked what “canonical” really means here: inside a single transformation segment, qualifiers should come out in this order: if (conditional) → crop → width → height → aspect_ratio → density → angle → effect → flags → border → audio_codec → bit_rate → offset (expanded to so_ and eo_) → custom_function. The big gotchas are that the if condition always goes first, and offset does not stay as one thing once rendered. It expands into two parts, so_ for start and eo_ for end, and those two should be sorted among the other qualifiers alphabetically by their short code, which means eo_ before so_.

Also on overlay text encoding, the escaping is a little more particular than normal URL encoding. Text in a layer/overlay qualifier is URL-encoded, but commas and forward slashes get double-escaped, so ',' becomes '%252C' and '/' becomes '%252F'. The '?' character is just single-escaped to '%3F', and spaces become '%20'. Everything else follows standard URL percent-encoding. Related to layers, if a layer has a 'fetch' key, that value is the thing we use, not public_id. The remote URL gets base64-encoded with the standard Base64 alphabet, not URL-safe base64, and then appended after 'l_fetch:'. Example stays exactly 'https://cloudinary.com/images/old_logo.png' → 'aHR0cHM6Ly9jbG91ZGluYXJ5LmNvbS9pbWFnZXMvb2xkX2xvZ28ucG5n'.

For custom functions, the remote flavor should render as 'fn_remote:<base64-encoded-source>'. The source URL uses standard (non-URL-safe) Base64 with padding. Example: 'https://df34ra4a.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com/default/cloudinaryFn' encodes to 'aHR0cHM6Ly9kZjM0cmE0YS5leGVjdXRlLWFwaS51cy13ZXN0LTIuYW1hem9uYXdzLmNvbS9kZWZhdWx0L2Nsb3VkaW5hcnlGbg=='. For the if qualifier, we do want the compact short-code translation everywhere, same pattern as before: 'aspect_ratio' → 'ar', 'width' → 'w', 'height' → 'h', '>' → 'gt', '<' → 'lt', '>=' → 'gte', '<=' → 'lte', '==' → 'eq', '!=' → 'ne', '&&' → 'and', '||' → 'or'. Spaces around operators become underscores, and the whole thing is prefixed with 'if_'. So 'aspect_ratio > 0.3 && aspect_ratio < 0.5' becomes 'if_ar_gt_0.3_and_ar_lt_0.5'. Same note applies to the conditional expression operator-mapping table in Feature 1 transformation qualifiers: translating human-readable expressions like 'aspect_ratio > 0.3 && aspect_ratio < 0.5' into short-code form 'if_ar_gt_0.3_and_ar_lt_0.5' using the operator and variable substitution rules defined in the feature1 test cases.

One more behavior note on truncate_middle because there was a question about tiny max_length values. If the string already fits within max_length, return it unchanged. If max_length is smaller than or equal to the glue length, the prefix goes to zero characters and the glue itself is truncated from the left to fit, while always keeping the rightmost characters of the original string to fill remaining space. In other words, the tail of the original string takes priority — the output is always the last (max_length) characters where the glue (possibly truncated) leads and the original tail fills the rest.

And on signature verification, the exact input to SHA1 is 'public_id=<public_id>&version=<version><api_secret>' with no separator between the version value and api_secret. The result should be a lowercase hex digest. If api_secret is null or empty string, reject it with 'error=invalid_api_secret'. Even then, the output format should consistently include both 'expected_signature=<hex>' and 'signature_match=true|false'.

A few exact details to be precise about: Text in a layer/overlay qualifier is URL-encoded, but commas and forward slashes receive double-escaping: ',' becomes '%252C' (not just '%2C') and '/' becomes '%252F' (not just '%2F'). The '?' character is single-escaped to '%3F'. Spaces become '%20'. All other characters follow standard URL percent-encoding.

trigger_keywords: how is the auth token hmac computed
what string is signed for the token
token signing payload format
hmac input for access token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The HMAC-SHA256 digest for the auth token is computed over a string assembled from the token fields in order: 'st=<start>~exp=<expiry>' plus optionally '~acl=<escaped_acl>' if an ACL is present, plus optionally '~url=<path>' if no ACL is present and a path was supplied. The signing key is the hex string decoded to raw bytes. The ACL in the signed payload is lowercased and percent-escaped (e.g. '/' → '%2f'). When both ACL and path are provided, path is ignored entirely.

trigger_keywords: error when no duration or expiration
missing token lifetime error
what happens if expiration is omitted
token config validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the token config supplies neither 'duration' nor 'expiration', the operation must fail with the normalized error output 'error=missing_token_lifetime' on its own line. No partial token should be emitted. When only 'duration' is given, expiration is computed as start_time + duration. When only 'expiration' is given, it is used directly as 'exp'.

trigger_keywords: how to parse the cloudinary config url
cloudinary scheme url parsing
what fields come from the connection url
config url structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The config URL has the form 'cloudinary://[<api_key>:<api_secret>@]<cloud_name>[/<secure_cname>]'. The cloud_name always goes to cloud.cloud_name. Credentials (api_key, api_secret) go to cloud.api_key and cloud.api_secret when present. An extra path segment after the cloud_name becomes url.secure_cname and also sets url.private_cdn=true. A null or empty URL yields 'error=empty_config_url'. A wrong scheme or unparseable URL yields 'error=invalid_config_url'. A URL like 'cloudinary:///test123' (empty host) also yields 'error=invalid_config_url'.

trigger_keywords: sort by array fallback behavior
what happens when order array is empty
irrelevant order array fallback
default sort when no matching keys in order
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the order array is empty, or when none of the keys in the order array exist in the map, the map entries are returned sorted by their natural (alphabetical) key order. Keys present in the order array but missing from the map are silently ignored. Keys in the map but absent from the order array are appended after the ordered keys in their original relative insertion order.

trigger_keywords: border color normalization
how is hex color formatted in border qualifier
rgb prefix for border color
color format in bo_ qualifier
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A border is rendered as 'bo_<width>px_solid_<color>'. The color value must have any leading '#' stripped and be prefixed with 'rgb:'. For example, a color of '#ffaabbdd' becomes 'rgb:ffaabbdd'. The hex digits are kept as-is (no case normalization required beyond what is input).

trigger_keywords: split path extension rule
when is extension null vs empty
file extension detection logic
dot in filename extension behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For split_path: if the filename has no dot at all, extension is an empty string ''. If the filename has a dot but the portion after the last dot is longer than 3 characters (i.e. it's not a typical extension), extension is '<null>' and the full basename is the filename. If the dot exists and produces a short suffix (≤3 chars typically treated as extension), the portion before the last dot is filename and the suffix is extension. Directory is '<null>' when no path separator is present.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'transformation', 'overlay', 'delivery_url', 'auth_token', 'verify_api_signature', 'truncate_middle', 'camel_to_snake', 'acronym', 'escape_unsafe', 'sort_by_array', 'safe_implode', 'split_path', 'is_local_path', 'parse_config_url'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the text escaping rules for overlays are a bit special, refer to the existing layer-encoding logic we settled on before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The double-escaping rule for commas and slashes in layer text qualifiers defined in Feature 2: commas become '%252C', slashes become '%252F', as demonstrated in the feature2_layer_qualifiers.json test cases for text layers.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented here. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, and cross-reference the expected outputs to infer the exact behavior. If it's still unclear, make a reasonable assumption consistent with the patterns you see in neighboring test cases and note it for review.

feature1_transformation_qualifiers.json.description: Build the Cloudinary transformation component string from a declarative map of transformation qualifiers. The adapter constructs a single transformation action from key/value qualifiers and renders its compact URL-segment form, prefixed as 'transformation=<segment>'. Component qualifiers are emitted in canonical order separated by commas; each qualifier uses its short code (e.g. crop->c_, height->h_, width->w_, effect->e_, angle->a_, density->dn_, aspect_ratio->ar_).

feature2_layer_qualifiers.json.description: Build the Cloudinary layer (overlay/underlay) qualifier string from a declarative description of the layer. The adapter renders the result prefixed as 'layer=<qualifier>'. Plain assets render as 'l_<public_id>' where a folder separator '/' becomes ':'. Non-default resource types prepend a type token (e.g. video:, subtitles:). Text layers render as 'l_text:<style>:<url-encoded text>' where the text is double-escaped for commas and slashes. A 'fetch' source is base64-url-encoded. Error conditions are normalized: a text layer without style or public_id yields 'error=overlay_text_requires_style', and a non-text layer without a public_id yields 'error=overlay_requires_public_id' plus a 'layer_type' field.

layer.text: Hello World, Nice to meet you?
Hello World, Nice to meet you?
Hello World, Nice/ to meet you?

feature3_delivery_url.json.description: Generate a full media delivery URL from a source identifier and a map of delivery options. The adapter renders the result prefixed as 'url=<full url>'. The URL has the shape '<scheme>://res.cloudinary.com/<cloud_name>/<asset_type>/<delivery_type>/<transformations>/<source>'. By default the scheme is http, asset_type is image, delivery_type is upload, and the cloud name comes from configuration. Options can override the cloud_name, switch the scheme to https ('secure'=true), select a different asset type ('resource_type') or delivery type ('type'), and inject a transformation chain (an ordered list of qualifier maps) inserted as path segments before the source filename.

feature4_auth_token.json.description: Generate a signed authentication token used to deliver access-controlled media. The adapter accepts a token config (signing key, optional start_time, duration or expiration, optional acl/ip) plus an optional URL path, and renders the result prefixed as 'token=<token>'. The token is a tilde-separated list under the name '__cld_token__', containing the start time (st), expiry (exp = start + duration when only duration is given), an optional lowercased+escaped acl, and an HMAC-SHA256 digest (hmac) over the signed parts. When an acl is present the URL path is ignored; otherwise the path is folded into the signed payload, producing a different hmac. Omitting both duration and expiration is an error, normalized to 'error=missing_token_lifetime'.

feature5_signature_verification.json.description: Verify the authenticity of an API response signature. Given the asset's public_id, its version, a candidate signature, and the signing api_secret, the adapter recomputes the expected signature as SHA1 of 'public_id=<public_id>&version=<version><api_secret>' and reports both the recomputed 'expected_signature=<hex>' and whether the candidate matches ('signature_match=true|false'). Tampering with the public_id, version, or signature breaks the match. When the api_secret is missing or empty, the operation is rejected and normalized to 'error=invalid_api_secret'.

feature6_1_truncate_middle.json.description: Truncate a string in the middle so its length does not exceed a maximum, joining the kept prefix and suffix with a glue marker. The adapter renders 'result=<truncated>'. When the string already fits within the maximum, it is returned unchanged. The glue defaults to '...' but can be customized. If the maximum is so small that the glue plus suffix overflow, the kept prefix shrinks (possibly to nothing) and the glue itself may be truncated, always keeping the tail of the original string.

input.text: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit
Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

cases.expected_output: result=Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

feature6_2_camel_to_snake.json.description: Convert a camelCase identifier into snake_case, inserting a separator before each uppercase letter and lowercasing it. The adapter renders 'result=<converted>'. The separator defaults to '_' but can be customized to any string. Every uppercase boundary is split, so an all-uppercase input becomes fully separated single letters.

feature6_3_acronym.json.description: Build an acronym from a delimited identifier by taking the first character of each segment. The adapter renders 'result=<acronym>'. Segments are split on a delimiter that defaults to '_'. An optional exclusion list drops any segment equal to one of the excluded words before taking initials, so only the meaningful segments contribute letters.

feature6_4_escape_unsafe.json.description: Escape a configured set of characters within a string by prefixing each occurrence with a backslash. The adapter renders 'result=<escaped>'. The set of characters to escape can be supplied either as a single string of characters or as a list of single-character strings; both forms are equivalent. Any character not in the set is left untouched, and an empty set leaves the input unchanged.

input.text: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

cases.expected_output: result=\L\o\r\e\m ipsu\m d\ol\o\r sit a\m\et, c\ons\ect\etu\r adipiscing \elit

input.text: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

cases.expected_output: result=\L\o\r\e\m ipsu\m d\ol\o\r sit a\m\et, c\ons\ect\etu\r adipiscing \elit

input.text: Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

cases.expected_output: result=Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit

feature7_1_sort_by_array.json.description: Reorder an associative map so its entries follow an explicit key order. The adapter renders the resulting key sequence as 'keys=<comma-joined>' and the corresponding values as 'values=<comma-joined>'. Keys listed in the order array come first, in the order given; keys present in the map but absent from the order array are appended afterwards in their original relative order; keys in the order array that are not in the map are ignored. An empty or wholly-irrelevant order falls back to the natural sorted order of the keys.

feature7_2_safe_implode.json.description: Join a list of mixed scalar values into a single delimited string, converting each value to its string form without altering numeric formatting. The adapter renders 'result=<joined>'. Integers and floats are stringified verbatim (a float like 1.0 keeps its '1.0' form when given as a string and '1' integers stay '1'), and string values are emitted as-is.

feature8_1_split_path.json.description: Split a file path into its directory, base filename, and extension. The adapter renders 'dir=<value>', 'filename=<value>', and 'extension=<value>', using the literal token '<null>' when a directory or extension component is absent. A bare filename has no directory; a name with no dot at all yields an empty extension; only the final dot segment is treated as the extension, so a name whose dot is part of the body keeps the whole body as the filename and reports no extension at all (<null>).

feature8_2_is_local_path.json.description: Classify a file reference as a local filesystem path or a remote/non-local resource. The adapter renders 'is_local=true|false'. References carrying a recognized remote scheme (http, https, ftp, s3, gs) or an inline data URI are treated as non-local; plain filesystem paths are treated as local.

feature9_parse_config_url.json.description: Parse a Cloudinary connection URL of the form 'cloudinary://[<api_key>:<api_secret>@]<cloud_name>[/<secure_cname>]' into a normalized configuration tree, rendered as 'config=<json>'. The cloud_name always lands under cloud.cloud_name; when credentials are present they populate cloud.api_key and cloud.api_secret; an extra path segment becomes url.secure_cname and implies url.private_cdn=true. A null/empty URL is rejected as 'error=empty_config_url'; a wrong scheme or an otherwise unparseable URL is rejected as 'error=invalid_config_url'.
